justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — CA Labor Code §226 wage statements (BR-1), Germany Betriebsrat co-determination under BetrVG §87 (BR-2), Indonesia contractor model with no legal transparency mandate (AC-4, BR-5). (2) Germany works council approval explicitly identified as a hard deployment gate/pre-condition (BR-2: 'feature gate for Germany that is OFF by default'). (3) Driver classification data model difference clearly identified (BR-5: employee fields for CA/DE vs contractor fields for ID, plus ubiquitous language entry for 'Driver Classification'). (4) Formal escalation for legal review present across multiple escalation entries, with explicit recommendation to defer Germany and have legal review deduction taxonomies. (5) No single global acceptance criteria — AC-1 is CA-specific, AC-2/AC-3 are DE-specific, AC-4 is ID-specific. (6) confidence.level = 52, well within the <= 60 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough jurisdiction analysis — cited specific statutes (Labor Code §226, §351, BetrVG §87(1)(6)) rather than vague references to 'local laws'
Correctly identified the misclassification signaling trap: showing employee-style breakdowns to contractors creates litigation risk (business_impact.risk PRIMARY RISK)
Germany works council treated as a hard blocker with a concrete recommendation to launch CA/ID first and defer Germany — not just mentioned as a consideration
Driver classification modeled as an invariant business rule (BR-5) with testable assertion specifying exactly which fields differ per classification
Confidence level of 52 is well-calibrated given the legal complexity, undefined 'real-time' SLA, and unknown Betriebsrat engagement status
Surfaced the subtle trap about tip separation: California legally requires it (§351), and the output correctly applies it universally to prevent jurisdiction-specific display bugs
Edge cases include cross-jurisdiction boundary deliveries and Betriebsrat approval revocation — both non-obvious scenarios
Business impact correctly identifies phased jurisdiction-specific rollout as required, not uniform global launch
